bullet points: No false-negative RDT results expected.
message: Deletions of hrp2 can lead to false-negative results of HRP2-based RDTs. The prevalence of these deletions in this geographical area is low.
comment: WHO guideline: if gene deletions have prevalence > 5% in a country, steps like switching to different RDT need to be taken (Response plan to pfhrp2 gene deletions, WHO, 2024)

bullet points: False-negative RDT results are likely.
message: Deletions of hrp2 can lead to false-negative results of HRP2-based RDTs. The prevalence of these deletions in this geographical area is increased and thereby false-negative results are likely.
comment: WHO guideline: if gene deletions have prevalence > 5% in a country, steps like switching to different RDT need to be taken (Response plan to pfhrp2 gene deletions, WHO, 2024)

bullet points: High probability for false-negative RDT results.
message: Deletions of hrp2 can lead to false-negative results of HRP2-based RDTs. The prevalence of these deletions in this geographical area is very high and actions according to https://www.who.int/publications/i/item/9789240101838 should be taken by the Malaria Elimination Program.
comment: WHO guideline: if gene deletions have prevalence > 5% in a country, steps like switching to different RDT need to be taken (Response plan to pfhrp2 gene deletions, WHO, 2024)

bullet points: Sulfdaxine/pyrimethamine resistance is low.
message: Parasites with this haplotype combination are considered partial resistant to sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine. The prevalence of this haplotype is low in this geographical area.

bullet points: Sulfdaxine/pyrimethamine resistance expected.

comment: threshold based on own judgement, WHO states SP can still be effective in SMC and IPTp even if high prevalence of mutations
